speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape three, November 26th, 1996, Helena Jonas Rosenzweig. You were-- you were telling us about the-- this one night in particular, when you first started working for Goeth.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was on that night. I stayed on. Helena went to sleep. Goeth had company. There were a lot of guest-- kapo, civilian, also in uniforms. There was a boy that used to be a servant, as well. His name was Liszec. And he was on with me that night. He used to polish his boats, prepare his clothes. Suddenly, it was almost 4:30 in the morning. And one of the civilian that we have seen very frequently coming to Goeth-- we knew him very well-- came out of the living room and said to Liszec he needs transportation to go home.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Liszec ran to the barn. And a carriage with the horse came to pick him up. The minute he left, Goeth walked out from the living room and said to Liszec, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] why did you go with my permission [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to the barn? I am the boss here, you know? The boy didn't have a chance to answer. He pulled out the gun and shot him right on the spot. And I was standing in the doorway. Could you imagine that? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] Him lying in blood, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] beautiful young boy, didn't even have a chance to explain.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Excuse me. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] At the same time, a villa was being built for Goeth, up on the main road, together with other houses for the other officers. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He ordered the two men that were in charge of the building of the villa to come down. One was the engineer and one was the man that executed the work to the workers. He said, why isn't the villa ready? And he ordered them to fight each other. He was standing there. I was near the window, where I-- from my kitchen. And the guys were fighting, and hitting each other, and he's shaking his head. He-- he wasn't satisfied. He stepped forward. He showed them how it's supposed to be done.

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- he was standing there and blood was flowing from their faces. That's where he turned around and raised his hand. It's OK. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Finally, the villa was built and we moved to the villa. It was a beautiful three-story villa. At that point, he said to both of us, from now on, you're not to go up to the barracks, because you goin' to bring germs and lice. You stay in the villa. And we had a room downstairs, next to the kitchen, that we shared.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in the other place when you first came to Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, right from the beginning, we came there in March 1943. I was only three days cleaning barracks. And then from after the three days, I was with Goeth.

speaker: interviewer
text: But how long were you in the other-- the other--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was like quite a few months. I don't know exactly the months-- but maybe-- maybe it was a month up on the hill and in the red house it was about five months, four or five months, till summer.

speaker: interviewer
text: So before you move to the villa--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were able to go and-- into the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah, we slept in the barracks, like other prisoners, like other people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like? You were-- were you with-- where were your sisters at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sisters were in different barracks. They were working. They were working for Madritsch, which they-- they were-- they were making uniforms for the German soldiers. So they were in different barracks. I was assigned to a different barrack to sleep. I was with Helen, Helen Hirsch. And I was in one in the barrack that I was assigned to. But this was only a short time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to see any of your family during that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, when I was-- but I was busy. I was working. I-- right away in the morning and at night-- every second night I stayed on to serve for the company he was entertaining. He was almost up every night, almost every night he would-- he would even sometimes just drink all night, if he wouldn't sleep. I had to be there. As long-- as long he didn't go to bed, I had to be on duty. One of us have to be on. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And many times, if he didn't sleep all night, he would get up like very early in the morning. It could be like, before 6:00, before people went to work. And this is when he went out killing, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] just-- just-- just like that. You know, he-- he was-- he was not sleeping. He would get up. I mean, he was-- he had a couple of different hats, you know, hanging right near the entrance of the-- of the-- of the villa. And you know, it's amazing that I knew what hat he would put on that he would be killing people. That's how attuned I was to this wild animal. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He would go early in the morning, like it must have been like, quarter to 6:00. And he would shot on the random. And the time, I knew that he did. Because when he came through the gate of the villa, he would whistle, like a happy whistle, like-- like I felt he's content. He accomplished something. And then he went to sleep. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It's very hard for me to talk about, especially about the time that I was with Goeth. Because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] since we moved to the villa, I wasn't allowed to go to the camp anymore. He told us never to go up to the camp. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: But you know, I did sneak out to the camp. I took my chances. Because my mother was alive. My sisters were there. And I had to see my mother. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when I came to see my mother, I had this phony smile on my face, you know, to pretend that everything is OK. But thinking back, how could she have believed me? Because knowing how people feared him-- you know that people actually, when they saw him from a distance, they were-- they were running away. And I found out they was-- they were hiding in the latrines, because they feared him so. He was-- he was a horrible creature. He was so cruel. He was so sadistic. He loved to see blood. He was a bloodthirsty animal.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] You know, he owned two large dogs. You know, every time there is a commercial on television now-- only yesterday, I saw they showing those black and white [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Danes. I turn my head. I can't look at them. And I love dogs. I don't know why I-- [INAUDIBLE] dogs what I saw what those dogs did to people. But that one-- he had two. One was like a beige and brown, Alf. And one was black and white, Ralf. He was so big that when I walked him, I had to bend my head. I brushed the dog. I fed the dog. He had somebody for-- especially for the dogs, to trim and to-- to clean them and to-- but I did feed the dogs. But those dogs-- he-- Goeth had a long glove, padded glove. He used to call me-- Susanna. He used to say to bring him that glove. And he would be in the back yard. And he told me to stay there. And he put that big glove on. He trained those dogs. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think this was the time when I was called for to testify against Goeth in Germany. And somehow, when I was telling this story, and I was talking about the dogs, all of the sudden, [SOBBING] I-- this scene came in front of me. [CRYING] [SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I blacked out. [SOBBING] I-- I really couldn't talk anymore. I just seeing how he would stay and give this order to the-- especially the the Ralf. He would say, Ralf! And the dog would run and tear people apart-- but tear 'em apart, would grab by parts of the body. And he would be standing there. I mean, I don't think I can ever forget it. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] [SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] [SOBBING] He hated. He was full of hate. Sometimes I remember this one particular time, when I was-- you know, he had a mistress living with him. He-- he lived the most luxurious life. He had a beautiful villa. There were weekly and daily delivery of fresh meat, fresh cold cuts, fresh fruit, fresh vegetables, wine, liquor. He had his mistress. Her name was Ruth. She lived with him there. And he had his two slaves. And I was one of them. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SOBBING] And this one day, when Ruth went to Krakow, to town-- which she frequently did go, in a closed up carriage, black carriage. And she would bring all kinds of things that she bought. She brought flowers. And she came down to the kitchen and arranged the flowers in a big vase. And the rest of them she left. And I took the rest of them, put them in a small vase. And I put them next to his telephone, on the first floor.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I don't remember exactly what I was doing around-- some of my chores-- when he was using the phone, making a telephone call. And he turned around to her. And he says, those flowers smell so nice. And she pointed at me. She says, Susanna did that. And I guess he was in a good mood that day, because he turned around, and-- with a pleasant expression, and said, so what? Even if she is nice, but she is a Jew, and I have to hate her. That's how deep that hatred was in him. What kind of a person was his mistress to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: She-- she was a very neutral, non-- you know, she didn't-- she didn't want to get mixed up in anything. To me she was one that lived with the luxuries. She beautified herself all day. All I saw her always lying with mask on her face. She always used to come down for egg whites and egg yolks-- do all kind of mixtures for her skin, to apply on her skin. She would go frequently to town, shop. She would come down, taste the food that we cooked. She had the music on. She didn't want to hear or know anything. Though one single time, I remember, one single time, she was in the kitchen. And somehow-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we got a lot of beating, and a lot of-- you know, he humiliated us in front of other people. He would call us name. He would call me, you stupid dog. You Jewish criminal. He would call me, you whore, and laugh. And they were all drunk. And they would laugh together with him. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And maybe, maybe she would feel a little bit sorry. I don't know. But one particular time, I recall her saying, with her hands out, if I could, I would send you home, but I can't. I don't know what she meant by that. I know it was after a dreadful night. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] [SIGHS] He said something to Helen. And she forgot to tell me. And she went to sleep. Because later-- I tell you later the story that we had to leave the villa. And I wasn't there. And she forgot to tell me. I guess it was a rough night for her. She forgot.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I came in the morning. And he gave me such a beating. He didn't know that she didn't tell me, and I wouldn't tell him that she didn't tell me. So he blamed it on me. I was thrown down the stairs. And then he came down the few steps. And he slapped me so many times. And I had to stand there, look straight in his face. You know how hard it was not to break down? You know how tough I became? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was like a rock.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I knew that he would kill me anyhow. Because he had this little gun. It was like a toy gun, a small one. And so many times, it was next to my skull. But somehow, I felt maybe it isn't time yet. It wasn't my time. Because actually, he was already comfortable with us. And maybe he-- I though maybe he doesn't want to train other maids. So maybe, when the time comes, he will finish us up. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] After a short time being in the villa, Oskar Schindler started to come as his guest. And right from the beginning, I notice he was rather different. He was always very happy. He drank a lot. But he was never drunk. He never was drunk, but he was always happy. His hands were always up when he talked. He was always center of everybody in the room.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other people were there besides him?

speaker: interviewee
text: All Gestapo. There were like, three civilians, four in uniforms. They were-- they were coming out of town. And there were maybe one or two from the camp, that were in charge, that were in charge of the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know any of the of the other Gestapo that were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. I remember first name being called out. But I don't really recall. I remember Schindler calling Goeth [? Moonie. ?] That was his nickname. Instead of Amon, he used to call him [? Moonie. ?] And they used to embrace a lot. And they used to be buddy, buddy. And it was hard to figure out-- when he would come down sometimes at night, when I was on duty, and he would put his hand on my shoulder, or pet my head. And he would call me [GERMAN]-- [GERMAN], because [GERMAN] means young child. Don't worry.  Don't worry. You will see. You'll be all right. And I was a little bit frightened. Because you know, at the time, when a German puts his arm or hand on-- on a Jew, on a Jewish girl, this is like Jewish-- like a race, you know. You're not supposed to be touched by a German. So they would kill you immediately. He wouldn't be at fault. You would be the one. So I was a little frightened. But his face was so gentle when he would say those words to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: He would always comfort me. And he asked me once about my mother, if I-- if I have a family. And at that time, my mother was sick already. She had pneumonia. And he said, do you need anything? And I was afraid to say. Because when somebody was ill in the camp, they were already not wanted. So she would be killed or sent away. But then when-- after a few times, when I saw him and he kept coming down, I did say that my mom is ill and she has pneumonia, and she-- he brought me once medication for her, but I was afraid to ask anymore. Because I felt maybe he will tell Goeth that my mother is sick and they will kill her. I wasn't sure who-- who Oskar Schindler was. I knew he was different. Did you know him by name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oskar Schindler, yes. He came a couple of times with his wife. She was a very, very subdued, dignified lady. But also, I saw him quite a few terms with other young women. And he would stay over. On the third foor there was a guest room. And he would also come sometimes during the day, play cards with Goeth. They were really very friendly. But-- but Oskar Schindler would always come under some excuse down in the kitchen-- for a cup of coffee, for another bottle of wine. And when he came down, he would always have something to say to me, to give me hope.

speaker: interviewee
text: As a matter of fact, he-- he even took me to the window, to look out where people were working down the road. He says, you see Susanna, you remember people in Egypt? They were slaves and then they were freed. He said, this is what's going to happen to you. One day, you're going  be all free from this hell. You will see. But it felt good. To be honest with you, it felt good hearing those words. But I wasn't quite sure what was going on. Because he was there with them. And I thought he was a Nazi, like the rest of them. Were you able to hear their conversations?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. The doors were closed. When they called me, there was laughter. There were musicians. Don't forget, there were always two musicians. When there were parties, they played all long-- all night long. But sometimes he would call them just to play. There were two of them, Rosners. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And the music was playing. And there were loud voices. But you couldn't hear. I guess a lot of decisions were being made in that room. I am sure. But what was happening, we were not allowed to listen. We're only serving. And when they needed something, he would call down. And I would come up, or she would come up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who would make the food for-- for him?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were cooking. That-- we had a cook for a while. You know, he was a man that used to. But most of the time, the mistress would come down. And she would help us to prepare things. She would come and taste the food, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would you eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I could eat anything. That-- that I had. And as a matter of fact-- as a matter of fact, only Poldek Pfefferberg, you know, reminded me that sometimes I would run out from the villa and bring sandwiches to the people they would march up to work. We did that. We took big chances by helping people with food, naturally. A lot of chances I took, a lot of chances, because I had to. I was in the position that I had to. You know, we go every year to the cemetery in Long Island, where we have monument with the names of our parents that perished in the Holocaust. Every year, we go for Yizkor. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Quite a few years ago, my sister was there. We were there. And she says, come, somebody wants to meet you. And a gentleman said to me, you know, I want you to meet my wife. And he said to his wife, you know, this young girl saved my life. You know, there was so many incidents like this that I don't-- didn't really recall. But this special one is because my sister was standing so proud. He said, you know, you were down in the office, in Goeth's office. Evidently, I was called to something, to bring something. I don't know. I think it were the keys-- or maybe it were the keys. Because I saw two men standing, two prisoners, and Goeth in front of a large glass cabinet, with all medical instruments. Evidently, those were the keys that I brought. Because suddenly, he said-- excuse me. You have-- excuse me. [DRINKS] He ordered them to move the cabinet to another room, down the hall, down the corridor. And he says, but if anything breaks, you will die. Supposedly, the man said, as soon as he-- as he turned to walk out from the door, you-- I wore a white apron always. You picked up that little apron, and you opened-- unlocked the door. And you scooped as many as you could into the apron. And you said to us, move it fast. Move it fast. He says, it was unbelievable, because it couldn't be possible to carry this cabinet without breaking the glass. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So it made me feel good to know that people remembered. Like another incident, where he told me to call the girl from next door, another house. And he said-- I'm standing in the-- the yard with her. And he said to her, tell your boss to whatever-- some order that he gave her.  And she says, I can't talk like this to my boss.

speaker: interviewee
text: He picked on  the gun, instantly. He says, who do you think you're talking to? Well, you know, I turned around and I stretched my hands, covered her. And I said, run! Run! And he-- he standing there, looking at me. You stupid dog. Why would you do that? But he-- he he put his gun back. What do you think he did that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think my time wasn't up. I think that it was meant for me to be here and tell the story.

speaker: interviewer
text: But why do you think he didn't kill you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because as I say, I think he was already very comfortable with the two of us. It would be maybe too much for him to train other slaves, other-- other maids. The camp was slowly, gradually, goin' to be liquidated. Because the Russian troops were moving closer to the borders of Poland. And I think that if he wouldn't be arrested toward the end, I wouldn't be alive, for sure. But somehow, it was God's will that he was arrested. And he didn't have a chance to kill us. Because he already killed a few people that knew a lot about him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hate him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think there is a word in the in the dictionary that could explain the fear and the enormous-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] hate is not the word. It's like I think that he was the most disgusting creature that-- that anybody could imagine. How could you-- I mean, he was so hateful. He was a murderer. He was a murderer. He enjoyed killing. I mean, if you would see his face after killing. He was a schizophrenic. He was-- he was-- his moods were changing. But at the same time, he enjoyed what he was doing. He didn't it as a order. He do it-- he did it out of pleasure. There was embedded hate in him.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what about in--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't like to use even the word hate. Because in spite of everything, I don't hate. Because hate brings evil. He was a evil man. The Nazis were evil people. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I'm scared of this word hate. I carry pain. Yes, I carry a lot of pain. But I don't want to hate. I don't want to hate. Because it's an ugly word. And-- and its related with evilness. They were evil. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] He [SOBBING] was standing in his room one day-- excuse me.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll continue on the next tape.
